definition_en: to make ashamed; to embarrass

wrong: To praise someone excessively.
To complete a task quickly and efficiently.

definition_en: to subside; to reduce

quiz_choices.correct: To lessen in intensity, severity, or amount; to subside.
wrong: To intensify or become stronger.

wrong: To secretly conspire against a ruler or government.

definition_en: something not typical; a deviation from the standard

wrong: A strong, unwavering belief or conviction.

Having a strong aversion or dislike for something.

wrong: To embrace something enthusiastically and without reservation.

quiz_choices.correct: Difficult to understand, especially due to being obscure or complex.
wrong: Easily understood and straightforward.
Having a strong, unpleasant smell.

famous_quote.text: His speech was full of clichés about hard work and success, making it feel unoriginal.

quiz_choices.correct: An overused phrase or idea that lacks originality.
wrong: A complex and unique literary device.
A sudden, profound realization.
A newly coined word or phrase.

definition_en: an exclusive group bound together by some shared quality or interest

famous_quote.text: The popular clique at school often excluded new students, making it hard for them to feel welcome.

etymology.root: cliquer (French: to click, to make a noise)

wrong: A sudden, sharp sound made by two hard objects striking together.
A type of elaborate knot used in sailing.

definition_en: to come together as one; to fuse; to unite

Easily persuaded or influenced by others.

The separation of different trades and employments from one another, seems to have taken place, in consequence of this advantage, in the most improved societies, and is generally deepest in those countries which enjoy the greatest degree of industry and improvement; that is, in those which are capable of the greatest multiplication of productions, and where, of consequence, a more comprehensive system of manufactures can be introduced.

famous_quote.text: The dogmas of the quiet past are inadequate to the stormy present. The occasion is piled high with difficulty, and we must rise with the occasion. As our case is new, so we must think anew and act anew. We must disenthrall ourselves, and then we shall save our country. Fellow-citizens, we cannot escape history. We of this Congress and this administration will be remembered in spite of ourselves. No personal significance, no personal insignificance, can spare one or another of us. The fiery trial through which we pass will light us down in honor or dishonor to the latest generation. We say we are for the Union. The world will not forget that we say this. We know how to save the Union. The world knows we do know how to save it. We -- even we here -- hold the power and bear the responsibility. In giving freedom to the slave, we assure freedom to the free -- honorable alike in what we give and what we preserve. We shall nobly save or meanly lose the last best hope of earth. Other means may succeed; this could not fail. The way is plain, peaceful, generous, just -- a way which, if followed, the world will forever applaud, and God must forever bless. This is the great dilemma before us.
